contexts.text: On 2025-04-03, Anayasa Mahkemesi struck down Article 7(3) as unconstitutional in case 2024/AYM-77.
Ephesus Biotech Inc. (EPBT) disclosed a clinical trial accrued liability of $7.8M in their FY2025 10-K filing.
Legal counsel noted that the outcome of case 2024/AYM-77 directly affects the valuation of Ephesus Biotech Inc.'s clinical trial accrued liability.

questions.query: Based on the Anayasa Mahkemesi amended ruling in case 2024/AYM-77, how would the clinical trial accrued liability of Ephesus Biotech Inc. (EPBT) be affected?
questions.ground_truth: The amended ruling (struck down Article 7(3) as unconstitutional) implies a reassessment of Ephesus Biotech Inc.'s clinical trial accrued liability from $7.8M to $14.2M.

contexts.text: In the Q2 FY2024 earnings call, Anatolian Technologies A.Ş. management projected net revenue of $42M with 18% YoY growth.
The audited Q4 FY2024 10-K filing for Anatolian Technologies A.Ş. (ANTK) reported net loss of $3.7M due to restructuring charges.
Sell-side analysts maintained their model reflecting net revenue of $42M with 18% YoY growth through Q3 FY2024, citing management guidance.

questions.query: Despite the Q2 forward-looking statement from Anatolian Technologies A.Ş. (ANTK), what was the actual financial outcome reported in the Q4 FY2024 earnings?
questions.ground_truth: The actual outcome was net loss of $3.7M due to restructuring charges, superseding the Q2 projection of net revenue of $42M with 18% YoY growth. The audited 10-K is the authoritative source.

contexts.text: Ephesus Biotech Inc. (EPBT) originally booked a clinical trial accrued liability of $7.8M in the preliminary FY2025 filing.
Following the year-end audit, the clinical trial accrued liability was restated to $14.2M per the final 10-K.

questions.query: What is the current clinical trial accrued liability for Ephesus Biotech Inc. after the FY2025 audit adjustment?
questions.ground_truth: The current clinical trial accrued liability is $14.2M, as restated in the final FY2025 10-K, replacing the preliminary figure of $7.8M.

contexts.text: Documentation (2018-07-01): The `np.matrix` module provides `np.matrix([[1, 2], [3, 4]])` for standard usage.
Changelog (2025-01-01): BREAKING — np.matrix class removed; use np.array or np.asarray. Deprecated in v1.15 → v2.2.0.
Tutorial (accessed 2024-12-01): 'To use numpy, simply call `np.matrix([[1, 2], [3, 4]])`.' — Note: this tutorial predates the v1.15 → v2.2.0 migration.

questions.query: A codebase uses `np.matrix([[1, 2], [3, 4]])` from the `numpy` library. According to the latest migration guide (2025-01-01), what is the correct replacement?
questions.ground_truth: The correct replacement as of 2025-01-01 is `np.array([[1, 2], [3, 4]])  # np.matrix fully removed`. The old `np.matrix([[1, 2], [3, 4]])` was removed in v1.15 → v2.2.0.

contexts.text: Documentation (2019-09-30): The `tf.contrib` module provides `tf.contrib.layers.fully_connected(x, 128)` for standard usage.

questions.query: A codebase uses `tf.contrib.layers.fully_connected(x, 128)` from the `tensorflow` library. According to the latest migration guide (2024-03-01), what is the correct replacement?
questions.ground_truth: The correct replacement as of 2024-03-01 is `tf.keras.layers.Dense(128)(x)`. The old `tf.contrib.layers.fully_connected(x, 128)` was removed in v1.15 → v2.16.
